3A_Bibliographic.citation: Xu, L., Potenza, M. N., & Bhatt, S. (2025). Beyond screen time: The core influences of problematic screen use on adolescent development networks. Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, Advance online publication.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: DOI prefix 10.1556/2006 corresponds to Journal of Behavioral Addictions. Senior author inferred as last listed.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Participants who completed both 2-year follow-up (T2, ages 11-12 years, 2018-2020) and 4-year follow-up (T4, ages 13-14 years, 2020-2022) were included to construct separate cross-sectional networks, and those who participated in T4 were included in the CLPN.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Cross-sectional networks: T2 n=9,054, T4 n=4,007. CLPN analyses: n=3,954.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time (video games, social media, TV/movies), phone-checking frequency, and problematic video game use (VGAQ), problematic social media use (SMAQ), problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ)
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ), Mobile Phone Attachment assessment, Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ)
3C_Exposure.modalities: video games, social media (social networking, texting, video chatting), TV shows/movies, mobile phone
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day for screen time; continuous scale scores for problematic use
3C_Exposure.reporter: Self-report (youth) for screen time; caregiver report for CBCL
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) assesses the duration of youth engagement in recreational activities. Based on responses to drop-down lists, the average daily screen time devoted to (1) video games, (2) social media (including social networking, texting, and video chatting), and (3) TV shows or movies were aggregated. The mobile phone checking frequency was obtained from the Mobile Phone Attachment assessment. Additionally, problematic video game, social media, and mobile phone use were assessed using the Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), the Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), and the Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ).

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: CBCL psychopathology subscales (anxious/depressed, withdrawn/depressed, somatic complaints, social problems, thought problems, attention problems, rule-breaking behavior, aggressive behavior), behavioral tendencies (UPPS impulsivity, BIS/BAS), and environmental factors (family conflict, peer involvement, school measures)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL), UPPS Impulsive Behavior Scale, BIS/BAS scale, Family Environment Scale, Peer Behavior Profile, School Risk and Protective Factors
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Caregivers provided annual reports on youth's psychopathological symptoms starting from baseline using the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL). Domains included anxious/depressed, withdrawn/depressed, somatic complaints, social problems, thought problems, attention problems, rule-breaking behavior, and aggressive behavior.
3D_Outcome.notes: Multiple outcome domains examined simultaneously in network analysis.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Cross-sectional Gaussian Graphical Models (GGM) with EBIC-gLASSO and Cross-Lagged Panel Network (CLPN) models with LASSO-penalized maximum-likelihood
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, race, ethnicity, parental marital status, parental highest education, family income
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: A Gaussian graphical model (GGM) was constructed using the Extended Bayesian Information Criterion (EBIC) in combination with graphical lasso (gLASSO) algorithms. A LASSO-penalized maximum-likelihood procedure, with 10-fold cross-validation for tuning parameter selection, was applied to shrink small coefficients to zero.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Network analysis approach; LASSO regularization serves as implicit multiple comparisons correction.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Higher problematic social media use at T2 correlated with increased somatic complaints (A2->C3, B = 0.302)
Problematic mobile phone use was associated with heightened impulsivity (A3->UPPS, B = 0.219)
heightened problematic social media use correlated with more behavioral activation (A2->BAS, B = 0.315)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: CLPN cross-lagged coefficient; negative = fewer symptoms
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: heightened problematic video game use correlated with fewer withdrawn/depressed symptoms (A1->C2, B = -0.248)

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper uses network analysis with LASSO regularization which does not produce traditional p-values for CLPN edges. CIs reported for cross-sectional centrality differences. The cross-lagged coefficients (B values) are mostly small but not contextualized as such.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Interventions and policies should prioritize addressing functional impairments related to screen use to foster healthier screen habits and mitigate adverse developmental effects among adolescents.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.population_override_verbatim: Although the impact of screen time may be relatively minor, it is important to recognize that small streams may contribute to the formation of seas. Thus, the cumulative effects of screen use should be considered, particularly given that screen time tends to increase with age.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Interventions and policies should prioritize addressing functional impairments related to screen use to foster healthier screen habits
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion argues that problematic screen use (characterized by functional impairment) is more central to adolescent developmental networks than screen time duration alone. It positions problematic use as a key factor with high out-strength predicting worse psychopathology and environmental outcomes. The paper explicitly states small time-based effects may accumulate and recommends policy action targeting functional impairments rather than time limits.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Network analysis coefficients are difficult to compare with traditional effect sizes. LASSO-regularized coefficients lack standard CIs and p-values for many edges. The paper reports B values for CLPN paths but these are not easily interpreted as standardized or unstandardized regression coefficients in the traditional sense.
